title: Product and Flaw Stages
narrationScript: Every discontinuity you will ever find has a birthday. It entered the metal at some specific point in the product's life, and knowing the stages of that life is the difference between searching at random and hunting with intent. We divide the life of a steel part into four stages. Inherent, primary processing, secondary processing, and in service. Each stage leaves its own signature flaws, in its own locations, with its own orientations. Learn the signatures and the part will tell you where to point your field. The inherent stage covers melting and solidification. Steel starts as a liquid full of dissolved gas and nonmetallic junk. As an ingot or casting freezes, that junk gets trapped. Nonmetallic inclusions are bits of slag, oxide, or refractory locked into the solid metal. Blowholes are pockets of gas that could not escape before the surface froze. Porosity is the scattered, finer version of the same thing. And as the last liquid at the top center of an ingot freezes and shrinks, it leaves a cavity called pipe. Mills crop the pipe off, but if any survives, it travels down the centerline of everything rolled from that ingot. Voids is the general word for internal empty spaces like shrinkage cavities. Inherent flaws mostly live inside the metal, so when they show at all, they show as broad, weak, fuzzy indications, and they often need a penetrating current to find. Primary processing means working the ingot into a mill product by rolling, forging, extruding, or drawing, and it does two things. It creates new flaws, and it transforms the inherent ones. Rolled flat, a surviving pipe or a patch of porosity becomes a lamination, a thin separation lying parallel to the plate surface. Remember that orientation. Flux flowing along a plate face passes right over a lamination, so magnetic particle testing will not find it on the face. It shows at cut edges and weld bevels. Inclusions get strung out into stringers, fine broken lines running with the grain. Surface defects on the billet stretch into seams, long tight crevices running the length of a bar. Metal that folds over and gets pressed into the surface without fusing becomes a lap, and because a lap meets the surface at a shallow angle with oxide trapped inside, it gives a weak, intermittent indication that is easy to dismiss and dangerous to dismiss. Forge too cold or take too big a bite and the metal tears inside. That is a forging burst, and it can be completely hidden under sound metal. And in heavy sections, dissolved hydrogen coming out of solution during cooling can split the steel internally into flakes. Secondary processing is the finishing work. Grinding done abusively overheats the surface and leaves grinding cracks, fine, shallow networks or parallel hairlines. Quenching during heat treatment can crack a part at section changes, keyways, and fillet radii, anywhere stress concentrates while the outside shrinks onto the hot core. Machining can tear the surface, and pickling or plating can charge the steel with hydrogen and crack it later. Then the part goes to work, and service adds the final stage. Fatigue cracks start at stress risers, fillets, threads, keyways, weld toes, and grow a little with every load cycle. They are tight, they are sharp, and they are the classic magnetic particle find during overhaul. Corrosion and stress corrosion cracking add their own damage in the wrong environments. So how do you use all this? Let the stage set the search. Overhauling a shaft, you are hunting in service fatigue, so concentrate on fillets and keyways and put your flux across them. Receiving plate for welding, check the cut edges for laminations before anyone strikes an arc. Inspecting a heavy forging, think about internal bursts and choose a current with penetration, half wave or full wave rectified, not straight alternating current. And always magnetize in two directions roughly ninety degrees apart, because a flaw lying along your flux lines is a flaw you did not test for. A discontinuity is just the metal's memory of something that happened to it. This lesson is about learning to read that memory before you ever squeeze the trigger.
imagePrompt: Inside a forge shop, a glowing orange steel billet is worked under a forging press while racks of cooled crankshaft forgings wait in the foreground for magnetic particle inspection.

sections.heading: A Discontinuity Has a Birthday
sections.body:
Every discontinuity entered the metal at a specific moment in the product's life, and that moment predicts almost everything you need to plan a test: where the flaw sits (surface or interior), how it is oriented (with the grain, across it, parallel to a surface), and what its indication will look like. CP-105 divides the product's life into four stages:

- **Inherent** — created during melting and solidification of the original ingot or casting.
- **Primary processing** — created or transformed while working the ingot into mill products: rolling, forging, extruding, drawing; casting defects carried forward belong here too.
- **Secondary processing** — created during finishing: machining, grinding, heat treatment, straightening, plating.
- **In-service** — created by operating loads and environment: fatigue, corrosion, hydrogen, overload.

Why care? Because a technician who knows the part's history hunts instead of wandering. An overhauled shaft means fatigue at fillets and keyways. New plate means laminations at cut edges. A heavy forging means internal bursts that need penetrating current. The stage also tells you which field direction matters most — and the rule from earlier modules still rules here: flux must cross a discontinuity at roughly **45–90 degrees** to throw a strong indication.

sections.caption: Discontinuity origin map — each stage of a product's life leaves its own signature flaws.

sections.heading: Inherent: Born in the Melt
sections.body:
**Nonmetallic inclusions** are slag, oxide, or refractory particles trapped as the melt solidifies. In the ingot they are blobs; after rolling they become **stringers** — fine, broken, parallel lines running with the working direction, at or just under the surface. **Blowholes** are gas pockets frozen into the ingot before the bubbles could escape; clean ones may weld shut during hot working, while dirty or near-surface ones persist as flaws. **Porosity** is the scattered, finer form of trapped gas, common in castings — mostly subsurface, producing faint, fuzzy, clustered indications when it shows at all. **Pipe** is the shrinkage cavity left at the top center of an ingot as the last liquid freezes and contracts; mills crop it, but any remnant travels down the **centerline** of every product rolled or forged from that ingot. **Voids** is the general term for internal empty spaces such as shrinkage cavities — subsurface by nature, showing only as broad, weak, fuzzy patches under penetrating current. Segregation — uneven chemistry frozen into the ingot — rounds out the stage, and it matters to MT mainly because local permeability differences can hold particles without any mechanical flaw.

Because inherent flaws are mostly interior, expect **broad, fuzzy, weak** indications, favor HWDC or FWDC, and treat centerlines and cut surfaces as prime territory.

sections.caption: Melt-stage flaws transform during hot working: pipe becomes centerline lamination, inclusions become stringers, billet surface defects become seams and laps.

sections.heading: Primary Processing: The Mill Leaves Its Marks
sections.body:
**Laminations** are pipe, porosity, or inclusions flattened when the ingot becomes plate — thin separations lying **parallel to the plate surface**. Flux flowing along the face passes over them without leaking, so MT cannot find a lamination on a plate face; they show at **cut edges and weld bevels**, which is why edge inspection before welding matters. **Laps** are surface metal folded over and pressed in without fusion — rolling laps in bar and threads, forging laps where flash and corners fold. A lap meets the surface at a **shallow angle** with oxide trapped inside, so its indication is weak, irregular, and intermittent — easy to dismiss, dangerous to dismiss. **Seams** are billet surface defects drawn into long, tight, **longitudinal** crevices in bar and tube — classic circular-magnetization targets. **Forging bursts** are internal or surface ruptures from working metal at the wrong temperature or with too much reduction per pass; internal bursts hide under sound metal and need penetrating current. **Flakes** are internal fissures in heavy rolled or forged sections, formed as dissolved **hydrogen** comes out of solution during cool-down — usually exposed only when machining cuts into them. Castings contribute **hot tears** (tearing at section changes as a restrained casting cools) and cold shuts.

The common thread: primary-processing flaws run **with the working direction**, so they demand a field across the grain — usually circular.

sections.heading: Secondary Processing and In-Service
sections.body:
Secondary processing turns near-finished parts into finished ones — and can crack them. **Grinding cracks** come from localized overheating under an abusive wheel: fine, shallow, often a crazed network or parallel hairlines roughly perpendicular to the grinding direction — tight and shallow, exactly what wet fluorescent excels at. **Heat-treat and quench cracks** form when quenching shrinks the surface onto a hot core; they start at **stress concentrations** — section changes, keyways, sharp fillets, even stamp marks — and can be deep and forked. **Machining tears**, straightening cracks, and **plating or pickling hydrogen cracks** round out the stage; hydrogen damage may not crack the part until hours or days after processing, which is why some procedures schedule MT a set time after plating.

In service, the part collects damage from load and environment. **Fatigue cracks** initiate at stress risers — fillets, threads, keyways, weld toes, corrosion pits — and grow with each cycle: tight, sharp-edged, strongly held particle lines, the signature MT find at overhaul. **Stress-corrosion** and **hydrogen-embrittlement cracking** appear in susceptible materials in the wrong environment, often as branched networks, and corrosion pitting roughens surfaces and seeds new fatigue. In-service flaws are surface-breaking far more often than not — exactly where MT is strongest.

sections.caption: The CP-105 discontinuity list: where each type originates and how it shows under magnetic particles.
rows: Rolled into stringers along the grain, surface or near-surface
Fine, broken, parallel lines; fuzzier when buried
Trapped gas in ingot or casting, usually interior
Soft rounded spots when near the surface
Internal hydrogen fissures in heavy sections
Short scattered indications on machined surfaces
Hot tears, quench, grinding, fatigue — at stress risers
Ingot top-center shrinkage → travels to product centerline
Broad centerline indication at ends, cuts, and bores
Invisible on faces; straight lines at cut edges and bevels
Shallow-angle surface fold with trapped oxide
Internal or surface ruptures in forged metal
Sharp if surface; broad and fuzzy if internal
Broad, weak, fuzzy patches under DC-type current

sections.title: Let the stage set the technique
sections.body: Overhaul work = surface fatigue at stress risers: continuous method, field **across** the fillet, keyway, or thread. Mill and forge work = interior melt-and-working flaws: **HWDC or FWDC** for penetration, dry powder on rough or hot surfaces. New plate = check **cut edges** for laminations before weld prep. And always two roughly perpendicular field directions — a flaw lying parallel to your flux is a flaw you did not test for.

figures.title: How melt flaws become product flaws

interactive.title: Match the Discontinuity to Its Stage
interactive.intro: Classify each discontinuity into the life stage where it originates.

items.label: Ingot pipe
items.why: Shrinkage cavity that forms at the top center of the ingot as the last liquid solidifies and contracts.

items.label: Nonmetallic inclusions
items.why: Slag, oxide, and refractory trapped during melting and solidification; later working strings them out along the grain.

items.label: Blowholes
items.why: Gas pockets trapped in the solidifying ingot or casting before the bubbles could escape.

items.label: Lap
items.why: Surface metal folded over and rolled or forged into the surface without fusing — a hot-working defect.

items.label: Lamination
items.why: Pipe, porosity, or inclusions flattened and elongated when the ingot is rolled into plate.

items.label: Forging burst
items.why: Internal or surface rupture from forging at improper temperature or with excessive reduction.

items.label: Hydrogen flakes
items.why: Internal fissures formed as dissolved hydrogen comes out of heavy hot-worked sections during cool-down.

items.label: Grinding cracks
items.why: Thermal cracking from localized overheating during abusive grinding — a finishing operation.

items.label: Quench crack
items.why: Cracking from thermal shock during heat-treat quenching, usually at section changes, keyways, and fillets.

items.label: Fatigue crack
items.why: Initiates at a stress riser and grows under cyclic service loads — the classic overhaul find.

checkQuestions.question: A longitudinal seam is suspected in hot-rolled bar stock. Which magnetization gives the best chance of finding it?
options: A coil shot, putting the field along the bar axis
A head shot, putting a circular field around the bar so flux crosses the seam
Either direction — orientation does not matter for seams
No magnetization — seams are found visually
checkQuestions.explanation: A seam runs longitudinally, so it needs flux crossing it at 45–90 degrees. Circular magnetization from a head shot wraps flux around the bar, square across a longitudinal seam.

checkQuestions.question: Why will MT performed on the face of a rolled plate fail to show a lamination?
options: The lamination lies parallel to the surface, so flux passes along it without leaking — check cut edges and bevels instead
Laminations are too deep for any NDT method
Laminations occur only in castings, not plate
Laminations are nonmagnetic and carry no flux at all
checkQuestions.explanation: A lamination is a thin separation parallel to the plate surface; flux traveling along the face crosses no interface and produces no leakage. Laminations show where they are cut through — at edges and weld bevels.

checkQuestions.question: Hydrogen flakes in a heavy forged shaft originate from:
options: Hydrogen absorbed from service environments years later
Dissolved hydrogen splitting the interior of heavy sections as they cool after hot working — a primary-processing flaw
Abusive grinding during finishing
Slag trapped during the original melt
checkQuestions.explanation: Flakes are internal fissures created when hydrogen comes out of solution during cool-down of heavy hot-worked sections, which places them in the primary processing stage.

checkQuestions.question: If the pipe cavity in an ingot is not completely cropped before rolling, the finished plate or bar will most likely contain:
options: Nothing — rolling always welds pipe shut
A centerline lamination or void running down the product
Surface porosity scattered across the faces
Grinding cracks at the ends
checkQuestions.explanation: Remnant pipe is flattened and elongated along the centerline as the ingot is worked, becoming a centerline lamination or void in everything made from that ingot.

checkQuestions.question: An internal forging burst is suspected just below the surface of a crankshaft forging. The best current choice is:
options: AC, because the skin effect concentrates flux at the surface
HWDC or FWDC, because rectified current penetrates while AC sees only the skin
The residual method with visible particles
Any current type, provided the amperage is doubled
checkQuestions.explanation: AC's skin effect limits it to surface flaws. Half-wave and full-wave rectified currents drive flux deeper, giving subsurface flaws like internal bursts a chance to leak enough field to show — typically as a broad, fuzzy indication.

references: CP-105-2024 MT Topical Outline §11.1–11.4 (inherent, primary, secondary, in-service stages)
CP-105-2024 MT Topical Outline §11.5 (types of discontinuities detected by MT)
Discontinuity guidance in practices such as ASTM E709 (generic reference)
